original_abstract: A rich tradition of older scholarship interpreted the Epistle of James as being addressed to the Jewish diaspora at large, regardless of their commitment to the Christian faith. While contemporary scholars have largely moved away from this perspective, I argue that the thesis warrants renewed consideration. Indeed, such a reading provides a framework for understanding several perplexing features of the text, including its conspicuous lack of explicit Christology, its deficiency in distinctive Christian sentiment, and its pervasive Jewish orientation. I propose that the author was a Jewish Christian who sought to maintain a viable presence within the synagogue and desired irenic relations with those who did not recognize Jesus as the Messiah. Consequently, the author was intentionally reticent regarding specific theological convictions for apologetic purposes—a tactical silence that finds clear precedents in other strands of ancient Christian literature.
humanized_abstract: From ancient times down through considerable stretches of modern exegesis the Epistle of James has been viewed as an invitation to Jews in the Diaspora, regardless of whether they became Christians or not. While this theory is hardly supported by contemporary scholarship, the thesis is worthwhile revisiting the thesis, not the least because it sheds important light on some of the puzzling factors in the letter, including the lack of Christology, a paucity of truly Christian sentiment and the pervasive Jewishness of the text. A Jewish Christian (albeit one who recognized Jesus as Messiah) was interested in having a life-giving missionary program which would enable him to exist in the synagogues and have amicable relations with the Jews who were unwilling to accept Jesus as Messiah. So the writer chose not to make any particular doctrine or item of doctrine the centre of the document, something which he could do in full awareness of numerous parallels from various other parts of the primitive period of Christian history.
